title: Abort: `assert(!PyErr_Occurred())` in `specialize` (`specialize.c:364`)
description: `Python/specialize.c`: the adaptive `LOAD_ATTR` specializer runs before the attribute load and never checks `PyErr_Occurred()`, so under OOM a stale `MemoryError` from an earlier allocation is still pending when it reaches `specialize(instr, LOAD_ATTR_MODULE)`, tripping the debug assert `!PyErr_Occurred()` (specialize.c:364) and aborting
notes: assert-based abort in the adaptive specializer. _SPECIALIZE_LOAD_ATTR (generated_cases.c.h:8761) calls _Py_Specialize_LoadAttr at the start of the LOAD_ATTR opcode, before the attribute is loaded; neither it nor specialize_module_load_attr_lock_held checks PyErr_Occurred(). Under set_nomemory, an earlier allocation leaves a MemoryError pending, so specialize() (specialize.c:364) and the type cache (typeobject.c:6342-6343) both hit their assert(!PyErr_Occurred()). Crashes on assertion-enabled builds: ft_debug_asan=abort, jit=abort; ft_release/upstream define NDEBUG so the assert is compiled out (n/a, clean exit -- specialized opcode is written with the error still pending, propagated normally). Confirmed crashing opcode 190 = LOAD_ATTR_MODULE; pending exception is MemoryError (gdb). Fix: bail out of specialization (unspecialize + return) when PyErr_Occurred() at the _Py_Specialize_* entry points, or don't invoke the specializer while tstate has a pending error. MINIMAL REPRO (shrinkray-reduced from the vehicle, then cleaned; deterministic, re-verified): `optparse.ngettext(0.0, 0, "")` (the `import asyncio` is load-bearing — it shifts the allocation window so a LOAD_ATTR specialization fails under OOM) under the set_nomemory sweep.
